questions.text: 7.6 grams of CS₂ is reacted with 12.8 g of O₂: CS₂ , + 3O₂ → CO₂ + 2SO₂limiting reactant ts:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The balanced chemical equation is;CS2 + 3O2 → CO2 + 2SO2Since molecular mass of CS2 is 12+ 64 =76g/molThe given mass is 7.6g,Hence number of moles of CS2 = 7.6/76 = 0.10 moles
The molecular mass of O2 is 32g/molThe given mass of O2 is 12.8gHence number of moles of O2 = 12.8/32 =0.13
﻿As mentioned in the above explanation, it is incorrect option
As mentioned in the above explanation, it is incorrect option

questions.explanation: The balanced chemical equation is; CS2 + 3O2 → CO2 + 2SO2 Since molecular mass of CS2 is 12+ 64 =76g/mol The given mass is 7.6g, Hence number of moles of CS2 = 7.6/76 = 0.10 moles The molecular mass of O2 is 32g/mol The given mass of O2 is 12.8g Hence number of moles of O2 = 12.8/32 =0.13 Since CS2 is taken in less amount, it is limiting reactant. Hence, option A is correct.

questions.text: The efficiency of chemical reaction can be expressed as:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The theoretical yield in chemistry refers to the maximum amount of product that could be formed in a chemical reaction, based on the stoichiometry of the reaction. It is the amount of product that would be obtained if the reaction proceeded to completion, with 100% efficiency, and no side reactions occurred.

In chemistry, the actual yield refers to the amount of product obtained from a chemical reaction in a laboratory or industrial setting. It is the quantity of a product that is actually produced or obtained when the reaction is carried out. The actual yield can be compared to the theoretical yield, which is the maximum amount of product that could theoretically be obtained under ideal conditions.

% yield is typically referred to as percentage yield. This is determined by dividing the number of moles of the product obtained by the number of moles of the 'limiting' reactant (the one present in the smallest amount), then multiplying by 100.
The term "maximum yield" is commonly referred to as the theoretical yield in chemistry.

questions.explanation: % yield is typically referred to as percentage yield. This is determined by dividing the number of moles of the product obtained by the number of moles of the 'limiting' reactant (the one present in the smallest amount), then multiplying by 100.

questions.text: Number of H2O molecules in 10 g of ice are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is correct.1 mole of any substance contains approximately 6.02 x 1023 molecules. So, 10 g of ice (H2O) contains roughly 3.34 x 1023 molecules.
This option is incorrect
This option is incorrect
This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: This is based on the concept of Avogadro's number, which states that 1 mole of any substance contains approximately 6.02 x 1023 molecules. So, 10 g of ice (H2O) contains roughly 3.34 x 1023 molecules.

questions.text: The same moles of H2, N2, and O2 are present in 0.1 cc volume at STP. Which one has the greatest number of molecules:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the number of molecules depends only on the number of moles, not on the type of gas.Avogadro’s law states that equal moles of gases contain equal numbers of molecules, regardless of their nature.Since all gases here (H₂, N₂, and O₂) have the same number of moles, they must contain the same number of molecules.Molecular weight or gas type does not affect molecule count in this case.Thus, N₂ doesn’t have more molecules than others.Hence, this option is wrong.

This option is also incorrect for the same reason.Even though H₂ is lighter, that does not change the number of molecules per mole.At STP, one mole of any gas occupies 22.4.If all three gases have equal moles, they have equal numbers of molecules.The molecular size or mass does not affect the molecule count per mole.Therefore, H₂ will not have more molecules than N₂ or O₂.So, this option is also incorrect.

The number of molecules is given by the formula:Number of molceules = moles x avogadros constantSince all of the given elements have the same number of moles all of them will have the same number of molecules, which will be 6.02 x 1023, the Avogadros constant.

This option is correct.By Avogadro’s Law, equal moles of all gases contain equal numbers of molecules under the same conditions of temperature and pressure.Here, H₂, N₂, and O₂ are all present in equal moles, so each will have the same number of molecules.Regardless of their atomic masses or molecular sizes, the molecule count remains identical.Hence, there is no difference in the number of molecules among them.Therefore, the number of molecules are equal.

questions.explanation: The number of molecules is given by the formula:Number of molceules = moles x avogadros constantSince all of the given elements have the same number of moles all of them will have the same number of molecules, which will be 6.02 x 1023, the Avogadros constant.

questions.text: Consider the reaction below, if 5 moles each of hydrogen and oxygen are reacted to form water, the reaction reveals:2H2 + O2 --> 2H2O
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Hydrogen (H2) cannot be the excess agent because it limits the amount of product formed, indicating that not all of it will be consumed.
This option is incorrect. Oxygen (O2) is not the limiting agent because there is enough hydrogen to react with the available oxygen, making it the excess reagent.

This option is correct. Hydrogen is the limiting reagent in this reaction, as it will be completely consumed before the oxygen, which means the production of water is limited by the amount of hydrogen available.

options.text: The reaction has no limiting reagent
options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In this reaction, there is a limiting reagent (H2), which will determine the maximum amount of product that can be formed.

questions.explanation: Option C is the correct answer. In the reaction 2H2 + O2 --> 2H2O, we start with 5 moles of both hydrogen and oxygen. To find the limiting reagent, we need to calculate how many moles of water can be produced from each reactant:Moles of water from hydrogen: 5 moles H2 * (2 moles H2O / 2 moles H2) = 5 moles H2OMoles of water from oxygen: 5 moles O2 * (2 moles H2O / 1 mole O2) = 10 moles H2OSince hydrogen can produce only 5 moles of water while oxygen could produce 10 moles, hydrogen is the limiting reagent. This means that all of the hydrogen will be consumed in the reaction before all the oxygen is used up, leaving some oxygen unreacted. Thus, the maximum amount of product, which is water, that can be formed is 5 moles.The other options are incorrect because:Option A: Hydrogen is not the excess agent; it is the limiting reagent.Option B: Oxygen is not the limiting agent; it is present in excess.Option D: There is indeed a limiting reagent in this reaction.

questions.text: 117.0g of NaCl have
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: To calculate the number of formula units of NaCl in 117.0g of NaCl, we can use the following steps: Calculate the number of moles of NaCl in 117.0g of NaCl. Multiply the number of moles of NaCl by Avogadro's number (6.022 x 10^23 atoms/mol) to get the number of formula units of NaCl. Step 1: Calculate the number of moles of NaCl in 117.0g of NaCl. The molar mass of NaCl is 58.5g/mol. This means that 1 mole of NaCl weighs 58.5g. Number of moles of NaCl = mass of NaCl / molar mass of NaCl Number of moles of NaCl = 117.0g / 58.5g/mol Number of moles of NaCl = 2.0 moles Step 2: Multiply the number of moles of NaCl by Avogadro's number to get the number of formula units of NaCl. Number of formula units of NaCl = number of moles of NaCl * Avogadro's number Number of formula units of NaCl = 2.0 moles * 6.022 x 10^23 atoms/mol Number of formula units of NaCl = 12.04 x 10^23 formula units Therefore, 117.0g of NaCl have 12.04 x 10^23 formula units of NaCl.

questions.text: What mass of CaCO3 gives 48.8 litres (measured at STP) of CO2, on strong heating?_.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: It is incorrect because the mass of CaCO3 that gives 48.8 liters of CO2 at STP is approximately 218.2 grams.
It is incorrect because the mass of CaCO3 that gives 48.8 liters of CO2 at STP is approximately 218.2 grams.

Write the balanced chemical equation: CaCO3(s) -> CaO(s) + CO2(g)Calculate the moles of CO2:At STP, 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 liters.So, moles of CO2 = 48.8 L / 22.4 L/mol = 2.18 molesUse the mole ratio from the balanced equation:1 mole of CaCO3 produces 1 mole of CO2. So, moles of CaCO3 = 2.18 molesCalculate the mass of CaCO3:Molar mass of CaCO3 = 40.08 g/mol + 12.01 g/mol + (3 * 16.00 g/mol) = 100.09 g/mol Mass of CaCO3 = moles * molar mass = 2.18 moles * 100.09 g/mol = 218.2 gTherefore, the mass of CaCO3 that gives 48.8 liters of CO2 at STP is approximately 218.2 grams.
It is incorrect because the mass of CaCO3 that gives 48.8 liters of CO2 at STP is approximately 218.2 grams.

questions.explanation: Write the balanced chemical equation: CaCO3(s) -> CaO(s) + CO2(g)Calculate the moles of CO2:At STP, 1 mole of any gas occupies 22.4 liters.So, moles of CO2 = 48.8 L / 22.4 L/mol = 2.18 molesUse the mole ratio from the balanced equation:1 mole of CaCO3 produces 1 mole of CO2. So, moles of CaCO3 = 2.18 molesCalculate the mass of CaCO3:Molar mass of CaCO3 = 40.08 g/mol + 12.01 g/mol + (3 * 16.00 g/mol) = 100.09 g/mol Mass of CaCO3 = moles * molar mass = 2.18 moles * 100.09 g/mol = 218.2 gTherefore, the mass of CaCO3 that gives 48.8 liters of CO2 at STP is approximately 218.2 grams.

questions.text: Which one is incorrect for actual yield being less than theoretical yield?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Mechanical losses – Incorrect. Mechanical losses occur due to inefficient transfer or handling of reactants, reducing actual yield.
Reversibility of reaction – Incorrect. If a reaction is reversible, some reactants may reform, preventing complete product formation.

options.text: All molecules do not possess activation energy
options.explanation: All molecules do not possess activation energy – Incorrect. Some reactant molecules may lack sufficient energy to overcome activation barriers, leading to incomplete reactions.
Substance is pure – correct. The purity of the substance does not affect actual yield since purity mainly influences reactant availability but does not inherently reduce yield.

questions.explanation: Substance is pure – correct. The purity of the substance does not affect actual yield since purity mainly influences reactant availability but does not inherently reduce yield.

questions.text: Which of the following is the carbon atom of a carbonyl group?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: sp hybridized – Incorrect. sp hybridization occurs in linear molecules like alkynes, not in carbonyl groups.
sp² hybridized – Correct. In a carbonyl group (C=O), the carbon is sp² hybridized, with one sigma bond and one pi bond.
sp³ hybridized – Incorrect. sp³ hybridization is found in single-bonded (tetrahedral) carbon atoms, not in carbonyl compounds.
dsp² hybridized – Incorrect. dsp² hybridization is observed in some square planar transition metal complexes, not in carbonyl groups.

questions.explanation: sp² hybridized – Correct. In a carbonyl group (C=O), the carbon is sp² hybridized, with one sigma bond and one pi bond.

questions.text: What is the weight of oxygen that required for the complete combustion of 3.2 kg of methane?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 3.2 kg: This is incorrect. It's simply the mass of methane, and doesn't account for the stoichiometry of the reaction.
6.4 kg: This is incorrect. It represents double the mass of methane, but doesn't correctly apply the molar mass of oxygen or the 2:1 ratio from the balanced chemical equation.

12.8 kg: This is the correct answer. Understanding the Chemical Reaction The complete combustion of methane (CH₄) with oxygen (O₂) produces carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water (H₂O). The balanced chemical equation is: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O This equation tells us that 1 mole of methane reacts with 2 moles of oxygen. CalculationsMolar Mass:Methane (CH₄): (12.01 g/mol) + 4(1.008 g/mol) ≈ 16.04 g/mol Oxygen (O₂): 2(16.00 g/mol) = 32.00 g/molMoles of Methane:Convert the mass of methane to grams: 3.2 kg * 1000 g/kg = 3200 gCalculate the moles of methane: 3200 g / 16.04 g/mol ≈ 199.5 molesMoles of Oxygen:From the balanced equation, 2 moles of oxygen are required for every 1 mole of methane.Moles of oxygen: 199.5 moles * 2 = 399 molesMass of Oxygen:Calculate the mass of oxygen: 399 moles * 32.00 g/mol = 12768 gConvert grams to Kilograms: 12768g / 1000g/kg = 12.768 kg.
15.4 kg: This is incorrect. It's a higher value than the calculated amount, and doesn't reflect the correct stoichiometric relationship.

questions.explanation: 12.8 kg: This is the correct answer. Understanding the Chemical Reaction The complete combustion of methane (CH₄) with oxygen (O₂) produces carbon dioxide (CO₂) and water (H₂O). The balanced chemical equation is: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O This equation tells us that 1 mole of methane reacts with 2 moles of oxygen. CalculationsMolar Mass:Methane (CH₄): (12.01 g/mol) + 4(1.008 g/mol) ≈ 16.04 g/mol Oxygen (O₂): 2(16.00 g/mol) = 32.00 g/molMoles of Methane:Convert the mass of methane to grams: 3.2 kg * 1000 g/kg = 3200 gCalculate the moles of methane: 3200 g / 16.04 g/mol ≈ 199.5 molesMoles of Oxygen:From the balanced equation, 2 moles of oxygen are required for every 1 mole of methane.Moles of oxygen: 199.5 moles * 2 = 399 molesMass of Oxygen:Calculate the mass of oxygen: 399 moles * 32.00 g/mol = 12768 gConvert grams to Kilograms: 12768g / 1000g/kg = 12.768 kg.

questions.text: When equal moles of reactants A and B are allowed to react according to the following balanced equation. (2 A+B→Product). The limiting reactant in this chemical equation will be?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Reactant A: This is the correct answer. Because the balanced equation shows that twice as much A is needed compared to B, having equal moles means A will be used up first.
Reactant B: This is incorrect. If B were the limiting reactant, we would need half the moles of A, which we have.
Reactant A and B: This is incorrect. There can only be one limiting reactant.
No Limiting reactant: This is incorrect. When the moles of reactants are not in the exact stoichiometric ratio, there will always be a limiting reactant.

questions.explanation: Reactant A: This is the correct answer. Because the balanced equation shows that twice as much A is needed compared to B, having equal moles means A will be used up first.

options.explanation: This option suggests that 3.0 g of carbon (C) on combustion produces 5.5 g of carbon dioxide (CO2). However, this is not correct because the molar mass of CO2 is 44 g/mol, and 3.0 g of carbon would produce less than 5.5 g of CO2.

This option suggests that 3.0 g of carbon (C) on combustion produces 22 g of carbon dioxide (CO2). However, this is not correct because the molar mass of CO2 is 44 g/mol, and 3.0 g of carbon would produce less than 22 g of CO2.

This option suggests that 3.0 g of carbon (C) on combustion produces 6.02 x 10^23 molecules of carbon dioxide (CO2). This is not correct because it represents Avogadro's number (6.02 x 10^23), which is the number of molecules in one mole of a substance. It does not directly relate to the amount of CO2 produced by the combustion of 3.0 g of carbon.

This option suggests that 3.0 g of carbon (C) on combustion produces 3.01 x 10^23 molecules of carbon dioxide (CO2). Similar to Option C, this is not correct because it does not provide a valid calculation or reasoning for the amount of CO2 produced.

This option suggests that 3.0 g of carbon (C) on combustion produces 1.505 x 10^23 molecules of carbon dioxide (CO2). This is the correct answer because it uses the correct stoichiometry and Avogadro's number to calculate the number of CO2 molecules produced by the combustion of 3.0 g of carbon.Explanation for Option E:When 3.0 g of carbon (C) is combusted, it reacts with oxygen (O2) to produce carbon dioxide (CO2) according to the balanced equation: C + O2 -> CO2.To calculate the number of CO2 molecules produced, we need to find the number of moles of C first. The molar mass of carbon is 12 g/mol.Number of moles of C = Mass of C / Molar mass of C = 3.0 g / 12 g/mol = 0.25 mol.According to the balanced equation, 1 mol of C reacts to produce 1 mol of CO2. Therefore, 0.25 mol of C will produce 0.25 mol of CO2.Now, to find the number of CO2 molecules, we can use Avogadro's number, which is approximately 6.02 x 10^23 molecules/mol.Number of CO2 molecules = Number of moles of CO2 * Avogadro's numberNumber of CO2 molecules = 0.25 mol * 6.02 x 10^23 molecules/mol ≈ 1.505 x 10^23 molecules

questions.explanation: 3.0g of C on combustion gives 1.505 X 10^23 molecule of CO2. As it is a numerical only one option E is correct.

questions.text: The amount of products that are actually produced during a chemical reaction by performing an experiment is called _.
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: A mole is a unit of measurement used in chemistry to express the amount of a substance. One mole of a substance is equal to its molar mass in grams and contains Avogadro's number of particles (approximately 6.022 x 10^23).

The actual yield is the amount of product that is obtained from a chemical reaction in an actual experiment. It represents the real amount of product produced under the given reaction conditions and may be different from the theoretical yield.

The theoretical yield is the calculated amount of product that should be obtained from a chemical reaction based on stoichiometry and assuming the complete conversion of all reactants into products. It is the maximum amount of product that could be obtained under ideal conditions.

The percent yield is the ratio of the actual yield to the theoretical yield, expressed as a percentage. It is used to assess the efficiency of a chemical reaction and is calculated using the formula: (Actual Yield / Theoretical Yield) x 100%.

questions.explanation: The mole is defined as containing exactly 6.02214076×10²³ elementary entities.2. The amount that is actually produced in a reaction is called the actual yield 3. The theoretical yield is the maximum possible mass of a product that can be made in a chemical reaction. 4. {Percent Yield} = (Actual Yield){Theoretical Yield} \times 100 \%

If we decrease the pressure, the reaction will lean toward the reverse direction. It will lessen the product, so the option is incorrect.

options.text: By increasing volume of reaction vessel
options.explanation: If we would increase the volume, the reaction will move in the backward direction, decreasing the yield. This option is incorrect.

options.text: By continous withdrawl of ammonia after intervals
options.explanation: Continual withdrawal of NH3 shifts the equilibrium to the right side to balance the effect of decreasing the amount of NH3 (Le Chatelier principle). Hence, this option is correct.

questions.explanation: Continual withdrawal of NH3 shifts the equilibrium to the right side to balance the effect of decreasing the amount of NH3 (Le Chatelier principle). Hence, option D is correct.

questions.text: By which of the following factors equilibrium state is attained earlier?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: While temperature can affect the rate of a reaction, it does not necessarily lead to an earlier attainment of the equilibrium state. Temperature influences the kinetic energy of the molecules, affecting the reaction rate, but it does not change the position of equilibrium. Equilibrium is reached when the forward and backward reactions occur at the same rate, regardless of temperature.

Similar to temperature, pressure affects the reaction rate but not the equilibrium state. Changing the pressure alters the concentration of reactant molecules and, consequently, the reaction rate. However, equilibrium is defined by the ratio of concentrations, not the absolute pressure.

Concentrations do play a role in establishing the equilibrium state. However, the equilibrium state is not necessarily reached earlier by changing concentrations alone. Altering the concentrations of reactants affects the rate at which equilibrium is attained, but it does not guarantee a faster attainment.

Catalysts are substances that increase the rate of a reaction without being consumed in the process. They provide an alternative pathway with lower activation energy. By reducing the energy barrier, catalysts speed up both the forward and backward reactions, allowing the equilibrium state to be reached more quickly. The presence of a catalyst does not alter the position of equilibrium but helps achieve equilibrium faster by facilitating the conversion of reactants to products and vice versa.

questions.explanation: Only using a catalyst can help attain equilibrium earlier as it speeds up the rate of both forward and backward reactions. This enhances the rate at which equilibrium is established.

questions.text: For the reaction given below:N2+ 3H2 -------> 2NH3How many moles of N2 are required for the synthesis of 4 moles of NH3?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

To determine the number of moles of N2 required for the synthesis of 4 moles of NH3, we can refer to the balanced chemical equation:N2 + 3H2 → 2NH3According to the stoichiometry of the equation, 1 mole of N2 reacts with 3 moles of H2 to produce 2 moles of NH3. This means that the ratio of moles between N2 and NH3 is 1:2.To find the moles of N2 required for the synthesis of 4 moles of NH3, we can set up a proportion:(1 mole of N2 / 2 moles of NH3) = (x moles of N2 / 4 moles of NH3)Cross-multiplying, we get:2 moles of NH3 * x moles of N2 = 1 mole of N2 * 4 moles of NH32x = 4Simplifying, we find:x = 2 moles of N2Therefore, the correct option is B) 2 moles of N2.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: 2 moles of N2 are required for the synthesis of 4 moles of NH3.

questions.text: One of the reactants consumed earlier than others in a chemical reaction is called:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Active ReactantThe reactant that actively participates in a chemical reaction.This term is less commonly used in stoichiometry but refers to a reactant that readily reacts under given conditions.

Excess ReactantThe reactant that is present in a larger quantity than necessary for the reaction to go to completion.Some of it remains unreacted after the reaction is complete.Example: In the reaction 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, if there is extra hydrogen gas after all the oxygen has reacted, H₂ is the excess reactant.

Limiting Reactant (Limiting Reagent)The reactant that is completely consumed first, stopping the reaction from continuing.It determines the amount of product formed.Example: In 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, if all the O₂ is used up first, then O₂ is the limiting reactant because no more water can form without it.
This option is not correct.

questions.explanation: In a chemical reaction, the limiting reactant is the reactant that is completely consumed or used up first, thereby limiting the amount of product that can be formed. The other reactants are present in excess, meaning there is more of them than needed to react completely with the limiting reactant. The limiting reactant determines the theoretical yield of the product in the reaction. Hence, option C is correct.

questions.text: In a chemical reaction, a limiting reactant is that:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The limiting reactant is the one that is consumed first.
The limiting reactant is the reactant that is completely consumed first, limiting the amount of product formed.
A catalyst speeds up a reaction but is not consumed.

options.text: Which is not taking part in the reaction but its presence is helpful in completion of the reaction
options.explanation: This describes a catalyst or an inert substance, not a limiting reactant.

questions.explanation: The limiting reactant is the reactant that is completely consumed first, limiting the amount of product formed.

questions.text: The total number of moles present in 2.8 g of an ethylene molecule are:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 0.1 g – Correct. The molar mass of ethylene (C₂H₄) is 28 g/mol. Moles = Mass / Molar Mass = 2.8 g / 28 g/mol = 0.1 moles.
0.2 g – Incorrect, as the calculated value is 0.1 moles.
1 g – Incorrect, this is an incorrect conversion.
2 g – Incorrect, as it is not the correct unit or value.

questions.explanation: 0.1 g – Correct. The molar mass of ethylene (C₂H₄) is 28 g/mol. Moles = Mass / Molar Mass = 2.8 g / 28 g/mol = 0.1 moles.

questions.text: 9.8 g of H₂SO₄ has been dissolved in excess of water to ionize it completely. How many H⁺ ions will it yield?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: 1.204 × 10²³ – Correct. The molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol. Moles of H₂SO₄ = 9.8 g / 98 g/mol = 0.1 moles. Each H₂SO₄ molecule releases 2 H⁺ ions, so total moles of H⁺ = 0.1 × 2 = 0.2 moles. Number of H⁺ ions = 0.2 × 6.02 × 10²³ = 1.204 × 10²³
1.204 × 10²⁴ – Incorrect, overestimated by a factor of 10
1.802 × 10²⁴ – Incorrect, incorrect calculation.
6.02 × 10²³ – Incorrect, this would be for 1 mole of H₂SO₄, not 0.1 moles.

questions.explanation: 1.204 × 10²³ – Correct. The molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol. Moles of H₂SO₄ = 9.8 g / 98 g/mol = 0.1 moles. Each H₂SO₄ molecule releases 2 H⁺ ions, so total moles of H⁺ = 0.1 × 2 = 0.2 moles. Number of H⁺ ions = 0.2 × 6.02 × 10²³ = 1.204 × 10²³

questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Incorrect as per explanations.

Number of H atoms in 36g of NH₄⁺:Molar mass of NH₄⁺ = 18 g/molMoles in 36g = 36 g / 18 g/mol = 2 molesEach NH₄⁺ ion has 4 hydrogen atomsTotal H atoms = 2 × 4 × Nₐ = 8Nₐ
